body.p:
A simple streaming stylesheet could look like this, for example:
UWE test footnote (22/40, callout V): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
body.pre: <xsl:stylesheet xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" version="3.0" exclude-result-prefixes="#all"> <xsl:output method="xml" indent="yes"/> <xsl:mode on-no-match="shallow-copy" use-accumulators="entry-count" streamable="true"/> <xsl:accumulator name="entry-count" as="xs:integer" initial-value="0" streamable="yes"> <xsl:accumulator-rule match="entry" select="$value + 1"/> </xsl:accumulator> <xsl:template match="/"> <result> <xsl:apply-templates/> <count> <xsl:value-of select="accumulator-after('entry-count')"/> </count> </result> </xsl:template> </xsl:stylesheet>
body.p: This stylesheet has some noteworthy features:

First, a default mode is declared that copies every node of the input instance to the output instance via an implicit identity transformation (shallow-copy) Mode options in XSLT3.0 Processing MethodsShallow Copy .
(The conventional way would be to use a template like this:)
Additionally, an accumulatorProcessing MethodsAccumulator is used.

messagepanel.typeofhazard: Normally, there are no variables in XSLT, only constants, as is also the case with functional programming languages.
messagepanel.howtoavoid: There has been a Saxon extension for some time that allowed multiple assignments of a value to a variable. In normal circumstances, however, this capability is not needed.

body.p: When processing very large data sets, however, assignable variables are indispensable, because otherwise the runtime stack would quickly reach its limits.
An accumulator accumulates values. These can be atomic types, as in the example above, but data structures can also be built up, such as the currently processed subtree in a dictionary.
The accumulator must have the streamable="yes" property set if it is to work in streaming mode. In this mode, the accumulator value can only be read once the examined tree has been fully traversed.

To identify the differences from "normal" XSLT operation, some changes can be made in the above example that the streaming processor will not accept. Saxon responds to this supposedly erroneous input with the following error message:
body.pre: Cannot call accumulator-after except during the post-descent phase of a streaming template

messagepanel.typeofhazard: This error message appears when you remove the [[code:apply-templates]] call. The accumulator is only filled when the tree has also been explicitly traversed.

body.p: This traversal can also be a pure copy, for example, the apply-templates call can also be replaced by a...
... which would be equivalent to the mode setting

As you can see, much has changed in XSLT 3.0 regarding the handling of different processing modes. Instead of writing default match rules, you set specific mode properties in the stylesheet declaration that realize the tree traversal in different ways.

messagepanel.typeofhazard: Processing large data sets with streaming is, however, somewhat tricky!
messagepanel.howtoavoid: It should always be checked whether a possibly conventional performance-optimized XSLT is sufficient for the use case.
